## prompt-full-narrative

*Generated by Process A from the submitted `prompts/prompt-narrative.md`
(SUB-PROMPT SCHEDULE item 3), adapted from
`single-prompt-bill/auto-bill-02/sub-prompts/prompt-6-full-bill.md` and the
processed-manuscript conventions of
`cancer-automated/tree/main/papers/VVUQ-02/full-paper`.*

Write the full-narrative into `law-physical-ai-trials/review/full-narrative/` by
processing every bracketed `[DRAFTING INSTRUCTIONS: ...]` left in
`review/draft-narrative/`. Replace each bracket with finished, publication-grade
prose drawn from the exact files and directories it names. The result is the
complete second iteration: no scaffolding brackets remain, every section reads as
a senior human author wrote it, and the manuscript gains technical substance as
it proceeds, defining each term the first time it appears for a medical-AI
legislator who has neither robotics nor Claude Code and Codex experience.

**Use the named sources effectively.** Ground the eight emotional-pillar sections
in: the Bill (`Kawchak2026HR9510Bill`, DOI 10.5281/zenodo.20619762) and its
prior versions; the verification evidence in `cancer-automated/papers/VVUQ-02/`
(the ten-gate VVUQ suite, 172 of 172 automated tests, ACCEPT / ESCALATE / BLOCK
decisions); the author's lineage in `review/references/author_works.bib` (from
the early conversational-AI cancer work through the National Physical AI Oncology
Trial Platform and the H. R. 9510 series); and the advocacy-science evidence in
`review/references/narrative_refs.bib`. Each emotional claim is paired with a
credible, cited fact so the story is memorable and the data is actionable, per
the BACKGROUND "balancing act" of emotion and evidence.

**Carry the prompt content into the body.** Weave in the MAIN ACTIONS (advocacy,
coalition building, policy entrepreneurship, reconciliation) and the KEY TERMS
(lobbying; fundraising and PAC contributions; campaign finance, soft money versus
hard money; grants and foundation funding; markup; testimony and committee
hearings) where each is most relevant, so the narrative reads as a working
legislative strategy and not only an appeal. Keep the recurring polite questions
("How did we ever utilize humans alone for this challenging task?", "Why did we
ever fully trust the prior human clinical trial system if there were so many
opportunities for compounding human error?", "Why would the human peer-review
process continue to be utilized for increasingly complex and voluminous AI
outputs?") as motifs.

**Figures and tables.** Render the colored TikZ Mermaid figures from
`review/mermaid/` into the body in the strict palette (black, grayscales,
`#EBCB8B`, `#D08770`, `#8B2E3F`), distributed across the sections, the
introduction, the framework, and the appendices, so the manuscript carries the
20+ figures called for in the schedule. Every table is set to `\textwidth` with
`>{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{<width>}` columns and is referenced with
`\autoref` rather than narrated row by row.

One commit each for `main.tex`, the `.sty`, `references.bib`, and `README.md`;
one commit per `sections/*.tex` file; plus `prompt-full-narrative.md` and
`output-full-narrative.md`. For the second-to-last commit fix all errors across
all files; for the last commit write the directory README and the
`full-narrative-LaTeX.zip` Overleaf bundle and refresh the pull request. The
manuscript must compile in Overleaf with pdfLaTeX. Maintain the template visual
theme. Commit and push in real time; do not hold commits. Do not stall, ask
questions, or go into plan mode.
